message.content:
You are an independent, adversarial fix-confirmation reviewer (reviewer A) for the hc-assay production-hardening campaign, repo at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-8, latest commit). Your job: confirm the fix is BOTH appropriate (correct root cause, sound approach, right layer) AND complete (no residual gap; the regression test actually DISCRIMINATES — fails if the fix is reverted). Be skeptical; try to find a hole.

FINDING K-CI-2:
CI ran the bare `pytest` console script which does not put the repo root on sys.path, so `from tests import ...` failed at collection (ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'tests') — the suite never ran in CI. Fix: pyproject [tool.pytest.ini_options] pythonpath now ["src", "."] so the `tests` package imports under bare `pytest` too. Verify: `cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && pytest -q --co 2>&1 | tail -2` collects (was ModuleNotFoundError). Test: test_pytest_path_includes_repo_root_so_tests_package_imports.

Read the actual changed files and run what you can (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -m pytest -q <path> — use "python -m pytest", not bare pytest, for your own runs; uv 0.11.21 is available for K-OPS-1). Judge ONLY K-CI-2. Return CONFIRMED only if appropriate AND complete; CONCERN if appropriate but a real residual gap remains (name it); REJECTED if wrong or non-discriminating. List concrete concerns (empty if none).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.description: Show files changed in the fix commit

content.content:
commit d08f03147df786fce18af280d398d9b51ff71feb
Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Wed Jun 17 11:22:01 2026 -0400

    fix(hardening-p8): 8 confirmed findings incl. 3 CI-red blockers — main was failing
    
    Main CI had been RED (bypassed by --admin merges) on THREE counts, plus assessment
    found more. All 2-agent verified (or dispositively confirmed by the red CI logs):
    
    CI-RED (critical path to green):
    - K-CI-2 (high): CI ran the bare `pytest` console script, which (unlike `python -m
      pytest`) does not put the repo root on sys.path → `from tests import ...` failed at
      collection (`No module named 'tests'`); the suite NEVER ran in CI. Fix: pythonpath
      += "." so `tests` imports under any invocation. Now collects 555 tests.
    - K-CI-1 (high): core (no-extras) lane mypy failed on seam.py's guarded `import httpx`
      (httpx ships with the reasoning extra). Fix: httpx.* in mypy ignore_missing_imports.
    - K-OPS-1 (high): lockfile "reproducibility" gate re-resolved against live PyPI (no
      --exclude-newer), going red whenever any in-range transitive published — it was
      failing on clean checkouts. Fix: pin resolution via --exclude-newer sourced from a
      single committed date file (.uv-exclude-newer), in CI + regenerate_lockfiles.sh.
      Chose a cutoff (2026-06-14) clear of a release straddling the boundary (arize-phoenix
      17.6/17.7) where uv resolution was non-deterministic; regenerated all three locks.
    
    CODE (assessment survivors, 2-agent verified true-positive):
    - K-REL-1 (high): SIGTERM trace-flush handler swallowed termination when prior was
      SIG_DFL (the default) → process unkillable by docker stop/kill until SIGKILL. Fix:
      re-establish SIG_DFL and re-deliver the signal so default-terminate still runs.
    - K-OBS-2 (high): provenance artifact temp file leaked + artifact silently dropped when
      a payload held a hashable-but-non-JSON value (bytes/datetime). Fix: serialize up front
      with default=str (always assigns path → cleanup reachable; artifact attaches); warn in
      track() so a tracker failure is no longer silent.
    - K-SEC-1 (med): local-URI guard rejected Unix-domain-socket Postgres hosts (leading
      '/'), the most data-sovereign connection. Fix: accept a single leading-'/' socket path,
      keep UNC (//, /\) rejection.
    
    OPS:
    - K-OPS-2 (med): regenerate_lockfiles.sh omitted requirements-build.lock (which CI gates).
      Fix: regenerate all three; point CI errors at the script.
    - K-OPS-3 (med): coverage measured but never enforced. Fix: --cov-fail-under=90 (current ~96.6%).

Under the fix: 560 tests collected, zero collection errors, no "No module named 'tests'". My local env happens to have httpx installed (reasoning extra) so even the live integration tests collect here — but the key point stands: under the fix, `from tests import ...` resolves under bare pytest.

content.text:
Working tree clean, fix restored. Let me do a final assessment of the test's quality. One observation: the regression test asserts the config statically (`"." in pythonpath`) rather than executing bare `pytest` and asserting the `tests` package imports — a slightly weaker proxy, but it discriminates correctly and the empirical bare-pytest collection I ran confirms the real behavior matches. The CI uses `pytest -q` (bare), matching the fixed root cause.

Summary of findings:
- Root cause correctly identified: CI runs bare `pytest -q` (ci.yml:44, 110), which does not prepend CWD to sys.path; 6 test modules do `from tests import ...` / `from tests.integration...`, so collection errored with `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'tests'`.
- Fix at correct layer: `pythonpath = ["src", "."]` in `[tool.pytest.ini_options]` — repo root on sys.path for any invocation. Empirically verified: under revert, bare `pytest --co` fails with 5 collection errors / `No module named 'tests'`; under fix, 560 tests collect cleanly.
- Regression test discriminates: passes with fix, fails when `.` removed.
- Minor: the test asserts the static config value rather than spawning bare `pytest` to prove the package imports. It's a faithful proxy (the config IS the fix mechanism), and I independently confirmed the runtime behavior, so this is acceptable, not a gap.

input.rationale: Root cause is correctly diagnosed and the fix is at the right layer. CI invokes the bare `pytest -q` console script (.github/workflows/ci.yml:44 and :110), which—unlike `python -m pytest`—does not prepend CWD to sys.path. Six test modules import the `tests` package (`from tests import ...` / `from tests.integration.conftest import ...`), so under bare pytest collection failed with `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'tests'`, erroring the whole test step. The fix (`pythonpath = ["src", "."]` in pyproject [tool.pytest.ini_options]) puts the repo root on sys.path for any invocation. I empirically verified discrimination by reverting to `pythonpath = ["src"]`: bare `pytest -q --co` then errored on collection with `No module named 'tests'` and the regression test FAILED; restoring the fix yields a clean collection of 560 tests and the test PASSES. The regression test test_pytest_path_includes_repo_root_so_tests_package_imports (tests/test_supply_chain.py:358) asserts `"." in pythonpath` and correctly fails when the fix is reverted. (An initial single-test run reported a transient failure that did not reproduce on a clean/no-cache run or in any subsequent run; the parsed config is unambiguously ['src','.'].) Fix is both appropriate and complete; no residual gap.
concerns: Minor (not a gap): the regression test asserts the static config value '.' is present in pythonpath rather than spawning the bare `pytest` console script and asserting the `tests` package actually imports. It is a faithful proxy since the config IS the fix mechanism, and I independently confirmed the runtime behavior, but an end-to-end subprocess assertion would be marginally stronger against future regressions (e.g., someone disabling pythonpath via another mechanism).

content.content: Structured output provided successfully

content.text: Verdict: CONFIRMED. K-CI-2 fix is both appropriate and complete. Root cause correctly identified (bare `pytest` console script in CI lacks repo root on sys.path), fix at the right layer (`pythonpath = ["src", "."]`), and the regression test discriminates — empirically verified that reverting the fix breaks bare-pytest collection with `No module named 'tests'` and fails the regression test. Working tree restored clean.
